fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a library that auto-generates Markdown reference docs from our provider schema definitions. Right now devs are hand-writing all the field documentation and it keeps going out of sync whenever schema changes — it's become a real maintenance headache. The idea is: feed in a schema (with all the attribute/block metadata), get back clean, consistently formatted Markdown. We want the same grouping logic we used in that routing/classification work from the config module — required stuff first, then optional, then read-only. Nested structures should expand into their own sub-sections with anchor links. Each field needs a compact inline label showing the type and any special markers (like if something is sensitive or going away soon). There are also some smaller text utility helpers we need bundled in — stripping formatting from Markdown text, prefixing lines, and wrapping file contents in fenced code blocks (with graceful handling when the file doesn't exist). The whole thing should be modular, not one giant file. Types, labeling, classification, schema rendering, and text helpers should all live separately. The JSON wire format for types supports both bare strings and nested arrays — make sure the rendering handles deep nesting correctly. Can someone take a look at the test cases in the rcb_tests folder and make sure everything lines up with those contracts?

One other pass with a few specifics the team asked for: for type names, the primitive mapping is very literal and limited to just 'bool' → 'Boolean', 'number' → 'Number', 'string' → 'String', and 'dynamic' → 'Dynamic'. Those are the only valid primitives, so anything else should be treated as an unsupported type error. For collections, list/set/map should read as 'List of <element>', 'Set of <element>', and 'Map of <element>' with the element rendered recursively. Object types render as just 'Object' and tuple types as just 'Tuple', without expanding members. Deep nesting should just stack naturally, like ['list', ['map', ['set', ['object', {}]]]] → 'List of Map of Set of Object'.

On the text helper side, plain_markdown should strip Markdown formatting markup while keeping the readable text itself intact. That includes heading markers (#), emphasis markers (* and _), inline code backticks, and list markers, and blank lines between stripped blocks should be collapsed so the result is plain readable text with no Markdown syntax. prefix_lines should add the provided prefix to every line, including the first one, and keep line breaks exactly as-is. The example to match is prefix '  ' on 'line1\nline2' yields '  line1\n  line2'.

A couple classification details were also worth locking down. If an attribute is both optional AND computed, it still lands in 'Optional', not 'Read-only'. 'Read-only' is only for computed when there is no optional flag set. And required beats everything, so if required=true, the label is 'Required' regardless of other flags. When markers are present, they come after the behavior word in a fixed order: 'Sensitive' first, then 'Deprecated'. They’re comma-separated, and only show up when that flag is true, so if the flag is absent or false, nothing extra is added.

Also, the package split should stay clean and explicit: (1) type rendering, (2) attribute/field label rendering, (3) behavior classification, (4) whole-schema markdown rendering, (5) text helpers (plain_markdown, prefix_lines, code_file), and (6) a thin execution adapter for JSON stdin/stdout. Core rendering logic should stay decoupled from I/O and JSON parsing. And for the classification behavior specifically, please make sure we line up with the classify_attribute and classify_block operations defined in feature6_1_classify_attribute.json and feature6_2_classify_block.json, which bucket fields into role=required / role=optional / role=read_only, along with the JSON test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/, specifically feature1 through feature7_3, which define the black-box contract for all ops dispatched through the execution adapter.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: func RenderSchema(schema Schema, w io.Writer) error

trigger_keywords: how is description text handled
what if description has leading or trailing spaces
description whitespace trimming
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The description is trimmed of leading and trailing whitespace before appending. It is appended outside the closing parenthesis, separated by a single space. If the description is empty or whitespace-only after trimming, nothing is appended after the closing parenthesis.

trigger_keywords: nested attribute label format
how does nesting_mode affect the label
difference between single and list/set/map nesting in attribute label
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For nested attributes: label opens with 'Attributes'. For nesting_mode='single', append the behavior word (Required/Optional/Read-only). For nesting_mode='list'/'set'/'map', append ' List'/' Set'/' Map' (with space) instead of a behavior word, then 'Min: N' only when min_items > 0, then 'Max: N' only when max_items > 0. Sensitive/Deprecated markers and trimmed description follow the same rules as scalar attributes.

trigger_keywords: how is a block classified as required vs optional
block behavior word for single nesting
what determines Required/Optional/Read-only for a block
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For a single-nesting block: 'Required' if min_items > 0; otherwise 'Optional' if the block is empty OR contains any required or optional descendant attribute/block (recursively); otherwise 'Read-only' if every leaf descendant is computed-only. For list/set/map blocks, no behavior word is appended — instead 'Min: N' when min_items > 0 and 'Max: N' when max_items > 0.

trigger_keywords: how are schema groups ordered
what is the section order in rendered markdown
required optional read-only heading order
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The three groups must appear in this fixed order: '### Required' first, then '### Optional', then '### Read-Only'. A group heading is only emitted when it has at least one member. Within each group, items are listed alphabetically by field name. The section begins with '## Schema' followed by a blank line.

trigger_keywords: how is the bullet item formatted in the schema section
does the top-level bullet include the behavior word
format of each field entry in the schema markdown
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each item in the schema section is rendered as a Markdown bullet: '- `name` (TypeLabel)' where the behavior word (Required/Optional/Read-only) is OMITTED from the inline label because the group heading already conveys it. The label still includes type, Sensitive/Deprecated markers, and description as usual.

trigger_keywords: what happens when a file is missing for code_file
file not found error format
how to handle missing file in code block helper
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the requested file cannot be read, the output must be a normalized error response in the format: 'error=file_not_found\nfile=<filename>\n' — not a generic error, not an exception, not empty output. The filename in the error must match exactly what was requested.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'write_type', 'attribute_description', 'nested_attribute_description', 'block_description', 'render_schema', 'classify_attribute', 'classify_block', 'plain_markdown', 'prefix_lines', 'code_file'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: graceful handling when the file doesn't exist
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The normalized error format 'error=file_not_found\nfile=<filename>\n' defined in feature7_3_code_file.json, not a generic exception or empty string

context_pointers.vague_reference: nested structures should expand into their own sub-sections with anchor links

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify directly — please check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the detailed PRD in start.md; the expected input/output pairs there are the authoritative contract for that behavior.

feature1_type_name_rendering.json.description: Render a Terraform value type into its human-readable documentation name. Primitive types map to Boolean/Number/String, the dynamic pseudo-type maps to Dynamic, collection wrappers prepend 'List of '/'Set of '/'Map of ' to their recursively rendered element type, structural object types render as Object and tuple types render as Tuple. The type is provided in JSON wire format.

cases.expected_output: List of Map of Set of Object

feature2_attribute_label.json.description: Render a single attribute's inline label. The label is wrapped in parentheses and begins with the rendered value type, followed by a behavior word derived from the attribute's flags (Required when required; Optional when optional, including optional+computed; Read-only when computed only), then optional Sensitive and Deprecated markers in that order, and finally the trimmed description text after the closing parenthesis. Surrounding whitespace in the description is trimmed.

cases.expected_output: (String, Required, Deprecated) This is an attribute.
(String, Required, Sensitive, Deprecated) This is an attribute.

feature3_nested_attribute_label.json.description: Render the inline label for an attribute whose type is a nested object collection. The label opens with 'Attributes' plus a collection word (none for single, List/Set/Map otherwise). For single nesting the behavior word (Required/Optional/Read-only) is appended; for List/Set/Map nesting the minimum item count is appended as 'Min: N' when greater than zero. A maximum item count is appended as 'Max: N' when greater than zero, followed by the trimmed description.

cases.expected_output: (Attributes List, Min: 2, Max: 3) This is an attribute.
(Attributes Set, Min: 5) This is an attribute.

feature4_block_label.json.description: Render the inline label for a nested block. The label opens with 'Block' plus a collection word (none for single, List/Set/Map otherwise). For single nesting the behavior word (Required/Optional/Read-only) is appended based on the block's minimum items and child characteristics; for List/Set/Map nesting the minimum item count is appended as 'Min: N' when greater than zero. A maximum item count is appended as 'Max: N' when greater than zero, then a Deprecated marker when the block is deprecated, and finally the trimmed block description.

cases.expected_output: (Block, Required, Deprecated) This is a block.
(Block List, Min: 1) This is a block.
(Block List, Min: 1, Max: 4) This is a block.
(Block List, Min: 1, Max: 4, Deprecated) This is a block.

feature5_schema_markdown.json.description: Render a complete provider/resource schema into a Markdown '## Schema' section. Attributes and blocks are partitioned into '### Required', '### Optional' and '### Read-Only' groups, each item rendered as a bullet with its inline type/behavior label, and nested object/block structures are emitted as linked sub-sections below. Input is a full schema document in JSON wire format; output is the rendered Markdown with trailing newlines trimmed.

cases.expected_output:
## Schema

### Required

- `route_table_id` (String)

### Optional

- `gateway_id` (String)
- `subnet_id` (String)

### Read-Only

- `id` (String) The ID of this resource.

feature6_1_classify_attribute.json.description: Classify a single attribute into the documentation grouping role used to bucket it under Required/Optional/Read-Only headings. A required attribute yields role=required, an optional attribute (including optional+computed) yields role=optional, and a computed-only attribute yields role=read_only.

feature6_2_classify_block.json.description: Classify a single nested block into the documentation grouping role. A block with a minimum item count above zero yields role=required; a zero-minimum block that is empty or contains any required/optional descendant yields role=optional; a block whose every leaf is computed-only yields role=read_only.

feature7_1_plain_markdown.json.description: Flatten Markdown source into plain text, stripping formatting markup (headings, emphasis, inline code, list markers) while preserving the readable textual content.

cases.expected_output:
Heading
Some bold and code text.

feature7_2_prefix_lines.json.description: Prepend a fixed prefix string to every line of a multi-line text block, including the first line, leaving line breaks otherwise intact.

feature7_3_code_file.json.description: Read a source file from disk and wrap its trimmed contents in a fenced code block tagged with the given format/language. When the file cannot be read, a normalized file_not_found error is produced naming the requested file.
